main_summary.summary: Robert Brown, born February 1935 in High Point, North Carolina, was interviewed October 1, 2013 by David Cline. Brown traces his upbringing by his grandmother Nelly Marshall Brown, a railroad maid descended from a slave sold at the Fayetteville slave mart, through his education at Leonard Street School and William Penn High School, where Mary McLeod Bethune mentored him on a school visit. After a year at Virginia Union and a year at North Carolina A&T, Captain Johnson recruited him to the High Point Police Department in 1956, where he was among four or five Black officers. He moved to federal law enforcement in New York City, testifying in the Vito Genovese mafia case before founding B&C Associates in December 1960 as a public relations firm. F.W. Woolworth Co. became his first major client; Brown counseled them through lunch-counter integration alongside John J. McCloy and Fred Kirby. Brown worked closely with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Rev. Wyatt Tee Walker of SCLC, underwriting movement expenses through corporate billings. He served as Special Assistant to President Richard Nixon (1968-1973), expanding Black general-officer ranks, federal contracts to Black businesses, and HBCU funding. Later he escorted Coretta Scott King to South Africa in 1986, met Nelson Mandela inside Pollsmoor Prison, and brought Mandela's grandchildren to Boston University.
key_themes: Black capitalism and minority business enterprise
main_summary.historical_significance:
Brown's account documents a rarely-recorded mechanism of civil rights change: corporate boardroom advocacy and PR consulting as a parallel track to public demonstrations. His first-person testimony connects SCLC field operations to retail-industry desegregation negotiations (Woolworth, S.H. Kress, A&P, Sealtest), naming specific intermediaries (Wyatt Tee Walker, John J. McCloy, Fred Kirby) and decision pathways. His four-year tenure as Special Assistant to Nixon, with cabinet-meeting attendance and direct presidential access, provides primary-source detail on minority business enterprise policy formation, Defense Department promotion review (the Chappie James generalship), and federal contracting expansion ($13M to $142M with Black businesses).

Brown's South Africa narrative — his 1986 visit with Coretta Scott King, his inside-prison meeting with Nelson Mandela at Pollsmoor (when Mandela had been unseen for over two decades), and the memo he carried to George Shultz and President Reagan documenting Mandela's terms for peace — constitutes a unique eyewitness record of pre-release diplomatic contact with Mandela by an American intermediary.

chapters.title: Grandmother and Family Roots
chapters.summary: Brown describes being raised in High Point by his grandmother Nelly Marshall Brown, whose father Joseph Kimmel Sr. was enslaved in Anson County and used as forced labor on North Carolina railroad construction. His grandmother brings him as a child to the old slave mart in Fayetteville where her father was sold, and to her childhood Rosenwald school in Wadesboro. Decades later Governor Jim Martin appointed Brown president and CEO of the North Carolina Railroad Company.
related_events: Brown's NC Railroad appointment by Governor Jim Martin

chapters.title: Segregated Childhood in High Point
chapters.summary: Brown, born February 1935, describes segregated water fountains and toilets in Woolworth and S.H. Kress stores. His grandmother corrects him for drinking from the white fountain, telling him God will fix it. Brown frames this story as bookend to his later corporate work with F.W. Woolworth Co. on integration, with John J. McCloy and Fred Kirby of the Allegheny Corporation on the Woolworth board.
related_events: Jim Crow segregation in North Carolina retail

chapters.title: William Penn and Mary McLeod Bethune
chapters.summary: Brown attends Leonard Street School through sixth grade and William Penn High School (founded by Quakers) for grades seven through twelve. The principal assigns him to escort Mary McLeod Bethune during a school visit; she counsels him for hours on hard work and life direction. Langston Hughes and Morehouse College professor Dr. Brailsford Brazeal also visit William Penn. Brown becomes a voracious reader, helped by neighbor Mrs. Loudon who brings him discarded newspapers from her white employer's home.
related_events: Brown's editorship of William Penn school paper

chapters.title: Becoming a Police Officer
chapters.summary: Brown attends Virginia Union for one year, returns home when his grandparents fall ill, and enrolls at North Carolina A&T. After taking the High Point police exam in 1956, Captain Johnson recruits him to the force. Brown describes arresting a white suspect in a creek branch and a public confrontation on Washington Street, breaking the unwritten rule that Black officers do not arrest white men. There were four or five Black officers in High Point at the time.
related_events: Brown's first arrests of white suspects in High Point

chapters.title: Federal Agent to B&C Associates
chapters.summary: Brown leaves High Point for federal law enforcement in New York City, working organized crime cases and testifying in the Vito Genovese mafia case. He resigns to pursue public relations, returning to High Point in December 1960 to found B&C Associates (initially with a partner named Campbell). Driving a 1960 Rambler, often sleeping in his car between meetings, he secures F.W. Woolworth Co. as his first major client in early 1961.

chapters.title: SCLC, Woolworth, and Corporate Pressure
chapters.summary: Brown meets Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Rev. Wyatt Tee Walker through Stanley S. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World. He underwrites SCLC hotel and movement expenses by charging them to corporate clients as business development. Brown works with Dr. Sidney H. Evans, A&T economist, to develop research on Black consumer purchasing power. He brokers integration of the National Retail Association lunch counters through Woolworth's influence and tests the The Peabody Hotel in Memphis personally before SCLC events.

chapters.title: 1968 and the Nixon Campaign
chapters.summary: Brown describes 1968: the King assassination, escorting Coretta Scott King to Memphis to retrieve King's body, the Hyatt Regency suite the night before the funeral with Sidney Poitier and Robert F. Kennedy. Brown assists the Poor People's Campaign planning, then works with Clarence Townes under Ray Bliss at the Republican National Committee. Bob Finch recruits him to the Nixon campaign; Brown joins the last presidential whistle-stop train tour across Ohio.

chapters.title: Special Assistant to President Nixon
chapters.summary: After the 1968 election, H.R. Bob Haldeman summons Brown to the Pierre Hotel transition office. Nixon introduces him to Herbert Brownell Jr. and William P. Rogers as a principal White House assistant. Brown tells the story of E. Frederic Morrow's marginalization under Eisenhower and Nixon's pledge of full access. Brown attends every cabinet meeting and is never overruled during his four years and three months at the White House. He drives expansion of Black general-officer promotions through reviewing Defense Department flag-officer lists with Haldeman.
related_events: Brown's appointment as Nixon Special Assistant
Defense Department flag officer promotion reviews

chapters.title: Coretta Scott King in South Africa
chapters.summary: Around 1986, Coretta Scott King asks Brown to arrange a South Africa trip through the King Center. Brown secures visas through South African ambassador Piet Koornhof. After overnight consultations with Dr. Allan Boesak and other South African leaders, Mrs. King decides not to meet with President P.W. Botha; Brown delivers her letter declining the meeting to the State House. The group then travels to Soweto to meet Winnie Mandela, who asks Brown to bring the Mandela grandchildren to America for school.
related_events: Coretta Scott King meeting with Winnie Mandela in Soweto

chapters.title: Meeting Mandela at Pollsmoor
chapters.summary: Niel van Heerden calls Brown at his hotel granting permission to visit Nelson Mandela at Pollsmoor Prison the next day. The chief warden welcomes Brown, Zenani Mandela, and Prince Moosa Dlamini and allows a two-hour visit when prior family visits had been capped at fifteen minutes. Mandela outlines terms for peace inside the country. Brown writes a memo immediately afterward and passes it to Winnie Mandela, the ANC, Secretary of State George Shultz, and President Reagan. Boston University President John Silber agrees to fund tuition for the Mandela grandchildren, who attend school in America for seven years funded personally by Brown.
related_events: Brown's 1986 visit to Mandela at Pollsmoor Prison
Mandela grandchildren's education at Boston University

chapters.title: Minority Business Enterprise
chapters.summary: Brown explains the Nixon administration's minority business enterprise program: organizing the National Commission on Minority Enterprise chaired by James M. Roche of GM, expanding Black auto dealerships, funding HBCUs at unprecedented levels, expanding the EEOC under William H. Brown III, and bringing James Farmer Jr. (head of CORE) into HEW as assistant secretary. Arthur A. Fletcher developed the Philadelphia Plan at Labor. Federal purchases from Black businesses rose from $13 million to $142 million during Nixon's tenure. Brown also visited military bases unannounced to address race-relations problems, working with Admiral John S. McCain Jr. (CINCPAC) in Hawaii.
related_events: National Commission on Minority Enterprise establishment

chapters.title: Sealtest, A&P, and Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Brown recounts intervening in a Cleveland Sealtest dispute when Dr. King could not get the dairy to meet with him. Brown flies to New York, meets the A&P CEO, and asks A&P to pressure Sealtest by threatening to pull Sealtest products from all Ohio stores. Within hours Sealtest contacts Dr. King; at a mass rally at Rev. Odie M. Hoover's Cleveland church Dr. King names Brown's role. Brown closes by thanking Lonnie Bunch and the Smithsonian NMAAHC board, noting he continues sending books to South African schools and over 300 libraries the program has established.
related_events: King's mass rally at Olivet Baptist Church Cleveland
